programs.name: Michigan Choice Waiver Program

eligibility.income_limits: Gross monthly income cannot exceed 300% of the Federal Benefit Rate (FBR)/SSI rate. 2025 limit: $2,982 (single applicant, excludes spousal income); prior years e.g., $2,910 or $2,523. Varies annually; no household size table specified as it's individual cap with spousal exclusions.[1][3][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: Countable assets limited to $9,950 (2025 figure, excludes one home, one car, and spousal protections under Federal Protected Spousal Asset guidelines). Home equity limit: $730,000 if intent to return (2025). 60-month look-back rule applies; assets given away/sold below fair market value trigger penalty period. Exempt: primary home (if equity ≤$730,000 or spouse/minor/disabled child lives there), one vehicle.[1][3]
other_requirements: Medicaid eligible
Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) determined via in-person Michigan Medicaid LOCD assessment (activities of daily living, cognitive, behavioral needs)
Require supports coordination plus at least one other waiver service
Live in home/community setting (not nursing home); available statewide since 1998

benefits.value: Medicaid-covered basics plus supports coordination and waiver services: adult day health, chore services, community health worker, community living supports, community transportation, counseling, environmental accessibility adaptations, fiscal intermediary, goods/services, home delivered meals, nursing services (preventative), personal emergency response systems (PERS), private duty nursing/respiratory care, respite, specialized medical equipment/supplies, training. No fixed dollar/hour amounts; individualized based on needs.[4][7]

methods: Contact local MI Choice Waiver Agency by region (phone interview for preliminary screening, then in-person LOCD)
Regional agencies listed on state site (no central online/mail/in-person specified; agency-directed)
forms: MI Choice Waiver Renewal Application (for renewals); LOCD tool for assessment[7][8]
documents: Income verification (e.g., gross Social Security before deductions)
Asset statements
Proof of residency, disability (if under 65), Medicaid status
Medical/functional need info for LOCD

geography.restrictions: None; available in all Michigan counties since 1998
geography.regional_variations: Administered by regional MI Choice Waiver Agencies/Area Agencies on Aging (e.g., Valley Area Agency: Genesee/Lapeer/Shiawassee; CareWell: Barry/Berrien/Branch/Calhoun/Cass/Kalamazoo/St. Joseph/Van Buren; The Senior Alliance: Wayne etc.; Region 1C: Wayne County via The Senior Alliance). Different providers, potential waitlist variations by agency/region.[2][4][7]
offices_or_providers: e.g., The Senior Alliance, 5454 Venoy Road, Wayne, MI 48184; contact local agency via state directory[7]

gotchas: Strict income cap (e.g., $1 over disqualifies; gross before deductions)[5]
60-month look-back penalty for asset transfers[1]
Must need NFLOC + supports coordination + 1 waiver service; dementia diagnosis alone insufficient[1]
Not for nursing home residents; community/home only[4][7]
Annual income/asset limits change (track 300% FBR)[3]
Regional agency required—must contact specific local provider, not central hotline[5][7]
programs.data_shape_notes: Administered via 10+ regional agencies with county assignments; eligibility has fixed individual income cap (not household-scaled) but spousal exemptions; benefits individualized by assessed needs/priority, not fixed hours/dollars; statewide but regionally varied providers/waitlists

programs.name: MI Health Link Program

eligibility.income_limits: No specific income dollar limits stated for MI Health Link itself; requires full Medicaid eligibility, which has income thresholds (e.g., for HCBS waiver context, applicant income limit equivalent to 300% of Federal Benefit Rate). People with Medicaid spend down are not eligible. Varies by household size per standard Medicaid rules, but no table provided in sources.[3][2]
eligibility.asset_limits: No specific asset dollar limits stated for MI Health Link itself; requires full Medicaid eligibility. For HCBS waiver context: Home equity interest no greater than $730,000 (2025) if intending to return home. Exemptions include spouse or dependent child(ren) living in home.[3]
other_requirements: Enrolled in both full Medicare (Parts A, B, and D) and full Medicaid benefits.

Live in specific Michigan counties: Barry, Berrien, Branch, Calhoun, Cass, Kalamazoo, Macomb, St. Joseph, Van Buren, Wayne, or any county in the Upper Peninsula (25 counties total, including Alger, Baraga, Chippewa, Delta, Dickinson, Gogebic, Houghton, Iron, Keweenaw, Luce, Mackinac, Marquette, Menominee, Ontonagon, Schoolcraft).[1][3][7]
Not residing in a state-operated veteran's home.
Not currently enrolled in hospice.
Not enrolled in PACE or MI Choice (must leave these programs to join).[2][8]
For HCBS: Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) determination via Michigan Medicaid LOCD, considering ADLs, cognitive abilities, and behaviors.[3][5]

benefits.value: Integrated Medicare and Medicaid benefits in one plan: medical services, behavioral health services, pharmacy (replaces Medicare Part D, no co-pays/deductibles on covered meds), home and community-based services (HCBS) including personal care (authorized for ADL level 3+), nursing home care, transportation, care coordination. One card for all services; care coordinator assists with plan, appointments, providers.[1][6][4][5]

methods: Passive enrollment: Eligible individuals receive letter from Michigan ENROLLS with ICO (Integrated Care Organization) options; no choice leads to auto-assignment.[1][7]
Opt-in for certain phases/regions (e.g., Macomb/Wayne Phase 2 starting April 2015).[7]

Contact health plan or Michigan ENROLLS (specific phone/website not listed; forms include Authorization to Disclose Protected Health Information (DCH-1183), Behavioral Health Consent Form (MDHHS-5515)).[1]
application.processing_time: Varies by enrollment phase; e.g., services begin May 1, 2015 for Phase 2 opt-ins (historical).[7]
forms: Authorization to Disclose Protected Health Information (DCH-1183)
documents: Proof of Medicare and Medicaid enrollment
Proof of residency in eligible county
LOCD for HCBS (if applicable; existing one may be used).[5]
HIPAA-related consents[1]

geography.restrictions: Limited to 25 specific counties: Barry, Berrien, Branch, Calhoun, Cass, Kalamazoo, Macomb, St. Joseph, Van Buren, Wayne, or any Upper Peninsula county (Alger, Baraga, Chippewa, Delta, Dickinson, Gogebic, Houghton, Iron, Keweenaw, Luce, Mackinac, Marquette, Menominee, Ontonagon, Schoolcraft).[1][3]
geography.regional_variations: Delivered via different Integrated Care Organizations (ICOs) or plans by region (e.g., HAP Empowered in Macomb/Wayne, DWMHA in Wayne). Moving out of service county terminates enrollment with notice; must link to new plan if available.[4][8][7]
offices_or_providers: ICO-specific providers (e.g., HAP for Macomb/Wayne, DWMHA for Wayne).[4][8]

gotchas: Must disenroll from PACE, MI Choice, or hospice to join; spend down Medicaid ineligible.[2]
County-restricted; moving out of eligible area ends participation.[8]
Nursing home residents pay patient pay amount.[2]
Auto-assignment if no choice made during enrollment window.[7]
Replaces Medicare Part D drug plan.[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: Dual-eligible only (full Medicare + Medicaid), county-restricted to 25 areas with regional ICO providers, requires NFLOC for HCBS, no spend down or conflicting programs allowed

programs.name: MI Options

eligibility.income_limits: No income or asset limits; open to all adults 18+, people with disabilities, Medicare beneficiaries, caregivers, and family members. (Note: Distinct from MI Choice Medicaid Waiver, which has income up to 300% of SSI/FPL for elderly/disabled.)[3][4]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits or tests apply.[3][4]
other_requirements: Michigan resident
No disability requirement for counseling access
Includes caregivers and family members seeking guidance on long-term care or Medicare

benefits.value: Free person-centered options counseling for long-term care (exploring home/community-based services, housing, transportation, caregiver respite, planning after life events); Medicare counseling (enrollment, plans, costs, scams); personalized action plans; sessions ~90 minutes with follow-up; provided by certified counselors via network of Area Agencies on Aging, Centers for Independent Living, nonprofits.[2][3][4][6]

methods: Phone: Statewide call center 800-803-7174 (8 a.m.-8 p.m. Mon-Fri) to speak with agent, schedule appointment, or get referral to local counselor[2][6]

Local providers: e.g., AgeWays (833-801-5587 or OptionsCounseling@AgeWays.org)[6]; WellWise Services (517-592-1974 or intake.referral@wellwiseservices.org)[7]; The Senior Alliance (via local AAA)[3][4]
application.processing_time: Immediate phone access; counselor appointments scheduled via call center (timeline not specified)
documents: No specific documents required for initial counseling; prepare personal goals, preferences, current situation for personalized planning

geography.regional_variations: Delivered through regional network of Area Agencies on Aging (16 AAAs), Centers for Independent Living, local nonprofits; local counselors provide community-specific guidance but core service uniform statewide[2][3][4][6][7]
offices_or_providers: Statewide call center; local: The Senior Alliance (Wayne County), AgeWays, WellWise Services (regional examples)

gotchas: Not a direct service provider or Medicaid program—provides counseling and referrals only, not benefits itself; often confused with MI Choice Waiver (which has slots, income limits, HCBS services); free but connects to potentially paid/eligibility-based programs; launched 2025, so verify current availability[1][2][3][5]
programs.data_shape_notes: no income/asset test; counseling/referral service via statewide call center and regional AAA/nonprofit network; distinct from entitlement-based MI Choice Waiver; open to all adults/caregivers regardless of finances

programs.name: Senior Project Fresh Program

eligibility.age: 60 years old or older (or 55+ if member of Michigan federally recognized tribe or urban tribal group)
eligibility.income_limits: Household income at or below 185% of Federal Poverty Guidelines. For 2024 (most recent data in search results): Individual $27,861/year ($2,321.75/month); Couple $37,814/year ($3,151.17/month); Household of 3: $47,767/year ($3,980.58/month); Household of 4: $57,720/year ($4,810/month). Note: Income limits are updated annually based on Federal Poverty Guidelines. Alternative income threshold mentioned in one source: $28,952.50 for individual, $39,127.50 for couple, but the 185% poverty guideline is the authoritative standard[1][2].
other_requirements: Must be a Michigan resident[1]
Must live in the county where coupons are being distributed[2]
Benefits must be obtained in the county in which the applicant lives[1]
Every eligible household member may apply separately[1]

benefits.value: $25 in coupons per eligible person that can be exchanged for fresh produce (fruits, vegetables, cut herbs, mushrooms, honey) at farmers markets and roadside stands[8]. Includes free nutrition counseling[8].
benefits.varies_by: household_size (each eligible household member receives coupons)

methods: Online application through Healthy Together platform (digital system as of 2025)[4]
In-person at local senior centers or community agencies[7]
Contact local lead agencies by county (varies by region)[2]
forms: Online application through Healthy Together app/website[4]
MDHHS-6011 (Senior Project Fresh Proxy Request form, if using a proxy)[3]
documents: Name[2]
Date of birth[2]
Gender[2]
Address[2]
Number in household[2]
Proof of income eligibility[2]
Confirmation of SNAP participation status (if applicable)[2]
Confirmation of other assistance programs (home delivered meals, commodities, congregate meal sites, etc.)[2]
Identification (required if using a proxy)[3]

geography.restrictions: Program is available in all 83 counties in Michigan[2]. Benefits must be obtained in the county where the applicant lives[1].
geography.regional_variations: Michigan State University Extension serves as a lead agency in 16 counties, providing nutrition education and coupon distribution. Other lead agencies serve remaining counties. Nutrition education delivery method varies by county: in-person, Zoom video/audio, or telephone[2]. Specific lead agencies and contact information vary by county[8].
geography.offices_or_providers: Multiple lead agencies by county; MSU Extension is primary in 16 counties; other community partners and senior centers distribute in remaining counties[2]

gotchas: Program runs May 1 – October 31 only; coupons expire on October 31 each year[2]
Online application opens May 1, 2026 (not available year-round)[1]
Income eligibility is based on yearly gross household income, and household is defined as people sharing an address and pooling income/expenses — this may include non-relatives[1]
If receiving coupons from MSU Extension staff, attendance at nutrition education class may be required (format varies by county)[2]
Transition to digital system (Healthy Together) as of 2025 means no more paper coupons or applications[4]
Farmers must grow Michigan-grown produce and sell only at farmers markets or roadside stands to participate[1][4]
Farmers must complete required Senior Project Fresh Farmer Training after applying[1]
If using a proxy to receive/redeem coupons, proxy must be at least 18 years old and provide identification plus written approval from the eligible person[3]
Income limits are updated annually based on Federal Poverty Guidelines — verify current year limits before applying
programs.data_shape_notes: This is a seasonal, county-based program with variable regional administration. Benefits are fixed ($25 per eligible person) but scale by household size since each eligible member can apply. Income eligibility is tied to federal poverty guidelines updated annually. The program transitioned to a fully digital system in 2025, eliminating paper-based applications. Nutrition education requirements vary by county and lead agency. The program is tightly geographically restricted — applicants must live in and receive benefits from their home county.

The program is tightly geographically restricted — applicants must live in and receive benefits from their home county."

## Key Takeaways for Families

**Timing is critical:** The 2026 season opens May 1 and closes October 31[1]. Applications are only available starting May 1[1], so families should plan ahead.

**Income is the main barrier:** Use the 185% Federal Poverty Guideline threshold to determine eligibility[2]. For 2024, a single person earning more than $27,861/year or a couple earning more than $37,814/year would not qualify, though these thresholds update annually[2].

**The benefit is modest but targeted:** Each eligible person receives $25 in coupons for fresh produce at farmers markets and roadside stands[8]. This is not a cash benefit—it's restricted to fresh fruits, vegetables, herbs, mushrooms, and honey[1].

**County matters:** Your loved one must apply in and receive benefits from their home county[1]. If they live in a county served by MSU Extension (16 counties), they may need to attend a nutrition education class, which can be done online or by phone depending on the county[2].

**The system is now fully digital:** As of 2025, all applications and coupon redemptions happen through the Healthy Together app/website[4]—no paper coupons or paper applications.

**Farmers can also participate:** If your family member is a farmer, they can register separately to accept Senior Project Fresh coupons at their farmers market or roadside stand[1][4].

programs.name: MiCAFE (Michigan's Coordinated Access to Food for the Elderly)

eligibility.age: Primarily seniors, though individuals with disabilities also qualify[4]
eligibility.income_limits: Based on income minus medical expenses[5]. Specific dollar amounts not provided in available sources. Eligibility determined through application process by Michigan Department of Health and Human Services[4].
other_requirements: Must be potentially eligible for SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program) benefits[3]
Household composition: all persons living together and purchasing/preparing food together are considered one household group[7]

benefits.value: Primary benefit is assistance accessing SNAP benefits via Michigan Bridge Card for purchasing food at authorized retailers[3]. Also assists with applying for utility assistance, tax programs, and other community programs[6]. Includes help paying for Medicare and Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Benefit for those who don't qualify for Bridge Card[1].

methods: In-person: Elder Law of Michigan, Inc., 3815 W. St. Joseph St., Suite C-200, Lansing, MI 48917[2]
In-home application assistance by trained, certified professionals where available[3]
forms: Authorization and Consent to Release Information form required[2]
documents: Personal identifying information (name, social security number, address, date of birth)[2]
Information about monthly income, shelter costs, medical expenses, and assets[2]
Information regarding current or past public benefits received[2]

geography.regional_variations: In-home application assistance available 'where available,' suggesting some regional variation in service delivery[3]
offices_or_providers: Primary office: Elder Law of Michigan, Inc., Lansing, MI[2]

programs.data_shape_notes: MiCAFE is primarily an application assistance and advocacy program rather than a direct benefit provider. The actual benefits come through SNAP (Bridge Card) administered by Michigan Department of Health and Human Services. MiCAFE's value is in helping eligible seniors navigate the application process and access benefits they might otherwise miss. Specific income and asset limits are not published in available sources — they follow SNAP federal guidelines as administered by Michigan DHHS. The program serves both elderly and disabled individuals. As of 2015, approximately 4,000 seniors were actively receiving SNAP through MiCAFE assistance[3].

**Key Context for Families:**

MiCAFE is **not a direct benefit program** — it's an **application assistance service**[3]. The actual food assistance comes through **SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)**, accessed via the Michigan Bridge Card[3]. MiCAFE's role is to help eligible seniors apply and navigate the system, particularly addressing the fact that nearly 50% of eligible seniors don't enroll[9].

The program was created specifically because barriers and stigma prevent eligible older adults from accessing benefits they qualify for[3]. If your elderly loved one is struggling with food costs and may qualify for assistance, MiCAFE provides free, confidential help with the application process[2].

**To get started:** Call **(877) 664-2233** Monday-Friday, 9 a.m. to 3 p.m., or visit **elderlawofmi.org/micafe**[1]. They can determine eligibility and assist with applications. Eligibility is based on household income minus medical expenses, which can be a significant factor for seniors with healthcare costs[5].

programs.name: Senior Companion Program

eligibility.income_limits: Limited income at or below 200% of the federal poverty level; exact dollar amounts not specified in sources and vary by household size per federal guidelines (e.g., consult current HHS poverty guidelines for table). No specific table provided for Michigan.
other_requirements: Meet income eligibility guidelines
Willing to serve a minimum of 15-20 hours per week (varies by provider: 15 hours in some, 20 in others)
Participate in pre-service orientation and monthly in-service training
Pass FBI fingerprinting and National Sex Offender background checks

benefits.value: One-on-one companionship and assistance with activities of daily living for adults 21+ with special needs (e.g., developmental disabilities, mental illness, frail health, dementia, physical/visual impairments, loneliness, isolation); provided by volunteer Senior Companions aged 55+ who receive tax-exempt hourly stipend, mileage reimbursement, meal assistance; service helps recipients maintain independence at home, with family, in group/nursing homes, or adult day care.

methods: Contact regional providers: e.g., Catholic Services of Macomb (phone not listed), Family Service Agency of Mid Michigan (810-257-3769), CCSEM Oakland/Macomb (248-537-3300, email lacommarec@ccsem.org), Milestone Senior Services (269-382-0515)
Referrals from non-profit/health agencies via Memorandums of Understanding
documents: Income verification to confirm eligibility at or below 200% poverty level
Background check materials for FBI fingerprinting and sex offender check

geography.restrictions: Serves 73 of 83 Michigan counties; specific providers in regions like Macomb, Oakland, Mid-Michigan, Kalamazoo area.
geography.regional_variations: Administered by local non-profits (e.g., CCSEM for Oakland/Macomb requires 20 hours/week; Catholic Services of Macomb 15 hours; different contact points and slight hour requirements; matching via local agency referrals.
offices_or_providers: Family Service Agency of Mid Michigan

gotchas: This is a volunteer program for low-income seniors (55+) to provide services to others, not direct benefits for the elderly loved one seeking care; families request matching via referrals, not direct enrollment
Income eligibility applies to the volunteer companion, not the care recipient (though recipients often frail/low-income)
No statewide centralized application; must contact local providers serving your county
Stipend for volunteers is tax-exempt and not counted as income
Not healthcare—focuses on companionship and non-medical ADL support
Limited to 73/83 counties; check local availability
programs.data_shape_notes: Federally-funded under Older Americans Act, locally administered by non-profits with regional providers and varying minimum service hours; eligibility for volunteer companions (not recipients); referral-based matching rather than direct family application
